title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Welcome
This guide teaches reproducible, collaborative data analysis workflows for the Horsley Lab at Yale. Our lab studies skin biology, wound healing, fibrosis, and stem cells — and increasingly, our science depends on computational analysis of complex datasets: single-cell RNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA-seq, and proteomics.
This handbook will help you get set up, learn the tools, and start doing real analysis — the right way from the beginning.

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Who This Guide Is For
This guide is for anyone joining the Horsley Lab who will be doing computational work. You don’t need prior experience with the tools we use — we’ll walk you through everything. If you’ve used R or Python before, great. If not, that’s fine too.

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
How to Use This Book
Start with Part 1: Quick Start. Install the tools, set up your computer, and run your first analysis. Every new lab member should begin here.
Parts 2–4 are reference material you’ll return to as needed:

Part 2: Core Tools — Deeper coverage of Positron, project organization, renv, and Git/GitHub
Part 3: Working with AI — How to work effectively with Claude Code as a coding collaborator
Part 4: Single-Cell Analysis — Horsley Lab-specific workflows for scRNAseq and spatial transcriptomics
Part 5: Workflows — Practical guides for common tasks

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Our Goal
Every lab member should feel confident doing computational work — not just running scripts someone else wrote, but understanding and building their own analyses. The tools in this guide are chosen to make that easier: a modern IDE, environment managers that keep projects organized, version control for sharing code, and AI assistants that help you write, debug, and learn faster.
Everything we do is oriented around one principle: your analyses should be reproducible and understandable by others in the lab.
Questions? Email valerie.horsley@yale.edu or ask in the lab Discord.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 What We Analyze
Welcome to the Horsley Lab. This chapter gives you a brief overview of how we work computationally before you dive into installation and setup.
The Horsley Lab studies skin biology, wound healing, fibrosis, and stem cells. Our computational work centers on:

title: 1  Introduction
text:
Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) — profiling gene expression in individual cells to understand cell types and states in skin and wound tissue
Spatial transcriptomics (Visium) — measuring gene expression while preserving tissue spatial context
Bulk RNA sequencing — comparing gene expression across conditions or genotypes
Proteomics and lipidomics — measuring protein and lipid profiles in tissues and extracellular vesicles

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.2 Our Tools
We use a standard set of tools for all computational work in the lab:



Tool
What it’s for




R
Statistical analysis, scRNAseq, plotting


Seurat
Single-cell RNA-seq analysis


Positron
Our IDE (coding environment)


renv
R package management


Git & GitHub
Version control and collaboration


Claude Code
AI coding assistant


Quarto
Writing analysis documents



You don’t need to know any of these yet — the next chapter will walk you through installing everything.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 How We Organize Work
Every analysis project in the lab follows a standard folder structure:
project-name/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/          ← original files, never modified
│   └── processed/    ← cleaned/transformed data
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_qc.Rmd          ← numbered in order of execution
│   ├── 02_preprocessing.Rmd
│   └── scratch/           ← exploratory files
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/
└── README.md
This structure makes it easy for anyone in the lab to understand and reproduce your work. You’ll set this up automatically using our /new-project skill in Chapter 3.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.4 Working with AI
We use Claude Code as an AI coding assistant throughout our work. It can help you write code, debug errors, understand scripts, and audit your analyses. We have custom lab skills that teach Claude our conventions — you’ll install these in the next chapter.






NoteA Note on AI and Learning



Claude Code is a powerful tool, but it’s not a substitute for understanding your analysis. Use it to help you write and debug code, but always make sure you understand what the code is doing and why. The /learn-code skill exists specifically to help you build that understanding.


Let’s get started with installation.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.1 Step 1: Find Out Your Mac Chip
This chapter walks you through setting up everything you need for computational work in the Horsley Lab. Do these steps in order.
You need to know whether your Mac has an Apple Silicon chip (M1/M2/M3/M4) or an Intel chip, because you’ll download different software versions for each.
Open Terminal (search for it in Spotlight with Cmd+Space, type “Terminal”) and run:
Write this down — you’ll need it for the next steps.

title: 2  Installation
text:
uname -m

arm64 → Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4)
x86_64 → Intel








NoteWindows users



Most of this guide assumes macOS. If you’re on Windows, the steps are similar but some commands differ. Where there are differences, both versions are shown. Ask in the lab Discord if you get stuck.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.2 Step 2: Install R
We use R for all statistical analysis and single-cell work.

Go to cloud.r-project.org
Click Download R for macOS (or Windows)
Download the version matching your chip (Apple Silicon or Intel)
Open the .pkg file and follow the installer

To verify it worked, open Terminal and run:
R --version
You should see something like R version 4.4.x.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.3 Step 3: Install Positron
Positron is our coding environment (IDE). It’s built for R and Python and has Claude Code built in.

Go to positron.posit.co
Download the version matching your chip (macOS Apple Silicon, macOS Intel, or Windows)
Open the .dmg file and drag Positron to your Applications folder (macOS), or run the installer (Windows)
Open Positron from your Applications folder

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.4 Step 4: Install Quarto
Quarto is what we use to write analysis documents that combine code, output, and text.

Go to quarto.org/docs/get-started
Download the macOS or Windows installer
Run the installer

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.5 Step 5: Install Claude Code
Claude Code is the AI coding assistant we use in the lab. There are two ways to install it — try Method A first.






Important



You’ll need an Anthropic account to use Claude Code. Ask Valerie if you need help getting access through the lab account.



2.5.1 Method A: Install via Positron Extensions (Recommended)
This is the simplest method and works directly inside Positron:

Open Positron
Click the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (looks like four squares)
Search for Claude Code
Click Install

Claude Code will appear as a panel in the Positron sidebar. This is where you’ll use it for all lab work.


2.5.2 Method B: Install via Terminal
If Method A doesn’t work, install via the terminal:

First, install Node.js from nodejs.org — download the LTS version and run the installer
Open Terminal and run:

npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

Verify it worked:

claude --version






Warningclaude command not found?



If you type claude in Terminal and get “command not found”, the install location isn’t in your PATH. Try:
npx @anthropic-ai/claude-code
If that works, ask in the lab Discord for help adding it to your PATH permanently.








Warningnpm errors?



If you get npm errors, make sure Node.js is installed first (node --version should return a version number). If Node.js is installed but npm still errors, try:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code --force
Or fall back to Method A (installing via Positron Extensions).

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.6 Step 6: Install the Lab Skills
Our lab has custom Claude Code skills that teach Claude our conventions.

2.6.1 Method A: Install via Positron sidebar (try first)
In the Claude Code panel in Positron, type:
/plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills
/plugin install horsley-lab-skills


2.6.2 Method B: Install via Terminal
If the Positron sidebar gives an error, open Terminal, type claude to launch Claude Code, then run:
/plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills
/plugin install horsley-lab-skills


2.6.3 Method C: Install manually (if Methods A and B don’t work)
If the plugin system isn’t working, install the skills directly:
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills
cd ~/.claude/skills
git clone https://github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills.git temp
cp -r temp/skills/* .
rm -rf temp
To verify the skills installed, run:
ls ~/.claude/skills/
You should see folders including audit-script, learn-code, new-project, and others.






TipSkills update automatically



If you installed via the plugin system (Methods A or B), skills update automatically when you restart Claude Code. If you installed manually (Method C), you’ll need to re-run the clone commands to get updates.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.7 Step 7: Install Git
Git is how we track changes to code and collaborate with others.
Check if you already have it:
git --version
If not installed, macOS will prompt you to install it automatically. Follow the prompts. On Windows, download Git from git-scm.com.
Then configure it with your name and email:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@yale.edu"

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.8 Step 8: Create a GitHub Account
If you don’t have one, create a free account at github.com. Use your Yale email address.
Then ask Valerie to add you to the Horsleylab GitHub organization.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.9 You’re Ready
Once all steps are complete, move on to Chapter 3 where you’ll create your first analysis project.






NoteGetting Help



If you get stuck at any step, ask in the lab Discord #resources channel or email valerie.horsley@yale.edu.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.10 Troubleshooting

2.10.1 claude command not found in Terminal
This means Claude Code wasn’t added to your PATH during installation. Try:
npx @anthropic-ai/claude-code
If that works, the install succeeded but the PATH wasn’t updated. Ask in the lab Discord for help fixing this permanently, or just use Claude Code via the Positron sidebar instead.


2.10.2 Claude Code not showing in Positron
If Claude Code isn’t visible as a panel in Positron after installing:

Go to the Extensions panel (Cmd+Shift+X / Ctrl+Shift+X)
Search “Claude Code”
Make sure it shows as Installed — if not, click Install
Restart Positron



2.10.3 /plugin command not recognized in Positron sidebar
Some versions of Claude Code don’t support /plugin in the sidebar — it only works in the terminal version. Try Method C (manual install) from Step 6 instead.


2.10.4 Plugin install fails with SSH error
SSH authentication failed
This means the plugin system is trying to use SSH to clone from GitHub but your SSH keys aren’t set up. Use the HTTPS version of the command instead — make sure you’re using:
/plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills
Not:
/plugin marketplace add github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills


2.10.5 Plugin install fails with “marketplace.json not found”
This is a plugin system configuration issue. Skip to Method C (manual install) from Step 6.


2.10.6 npm errors during Claude Code install
Make sure Node.js is installed first:
node --version
If you get “command not found”, install Node.js from nodejs.org first, then retry the npm install.


2.10.7 Nothing works
Install Claude Code via the Positron Extensions panel (Step 5, Method A) and install the lab skills manually (Step 6, Method C). This combination bypasses all the terminal and PATH issues and works on any machine.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.1 Open Positron
In this chapter you’ll create your first analysis project using the Horsley Lab conventions. We’ll use the /new-project skill to set everything up automatically.
Open Positron from your Applications folder. You’ll see a layout similar to RStudio but more modern:

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
Left sidebar — file explorer, git, extensions
Main area — code editor
Bottom panel — terminal, console, output
Right sidebar — Claude Code chat panel

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.2 Create a Project Folder
First, create a folder for your project. In Terminal:
mkdir ~/projects/my-first-project
cd ~/projects/my-first-project
Then in Positron, go to File → Open Folder and open my-first-project.

title: 3  Your First R Project
section: 3.3 Use /new-project to Set Up the Structure
text:
3.3 Use /new-project to Set Up the Structure
In the Claude Code sidebar in Positron, type:
/new-project
Claude will ask you a few questions:

Project type — choose Data science
Project name — my-first-project (or whatever you chose)
Brief description — one sentence about what this project is for
Python needed? — No for now
Layout — Flat
Cluster? — No for now
GitHub — Personal account, Private

Claude Code will then create the full folder structure, initialize renv, create a README, CLAUDE.md, and set up git.






Tip



The /new-project skill sets up everything according to Horsley Lab conventions automatically. You don’t need to create folders manually.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.4 Explore the Structure
After /new-project finishes, you should see:
my-first-project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   └── processed/
├── scripts/
│   └── scratch/
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/
├── .claude/
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── renv/
├── renv.lock
└── README.md

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.5 Create Your First Script
In Positron, create a new file called 01_import.Rmd in the scripts/ folder.
Add this content:
---
title: "01 — Import and Explore Data"
date: "2026-04-21"
output: html_document
---



## Load Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# We'll add data loading here
cat("Script is working!\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Script is working!
```


:::
:::

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.6 Run the Script
Click the Run button next to any code chunk, or use Cmd+Shift+Enter to run the current chunk. You should see “Script is working!” in the console.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.7 Use /done to Wrap Up
At the end of any working session, type in the Claude Code sidebar:
/done
This will summarize what you did, update your CLAUDE.md session log, and commit your work to git.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.8 Next Steps
You now have a working project with the correct Horsley Lab structure. In the next chapter, you’ll learn how to work with Claude Code day-to-day to help you write and understand code.

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.1 What Claude Code Can Do
Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built into Positron. This chapter covers how to use it effectively for day-to-day analysis work in the lab.
Claude Code can help you:

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
Write code — describe what you want and it writes the R or Python
Debug errors — paste an error message and it explains what went wrong and how to fix it
Understand code — use /learn-code to walk through any script section by section
Audit scripts — use /audit-script before submitting work for review
Set up projects — use /new-project to scaffold a new analysis
Wrap up sessions — use /done to commit work and update your session log

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.2 The Lab Skills
When you installed the Horsley Lab skills, you gave Claude Code knowledge of our specific conventions. This means Claude already knows:

Our folder structure (data/raw/, scripts/scratch/, outputs/figures/)
Seurat-specific best practices (checking active assay, tracking cell counts, etc.)
How to audit an R script for common single-cell analysis mistakes
How to set up a new project the Horsley Lab way

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
section: 4.3 How to Ask for Help Effectively
text:
4.3 How to Ask for Help Effectively
The more context you give Claude Code, the better it can help. Instead of:

“My code isn’t working”

Try:

“I’m trying to run FindMarkers() on my Seurat object but getting an error that says ‘object of type closure is not subsettable’. Here’s my code: [paste code]”

Good things to include: - The exact error message - The relevant code - What you were trying to do - What data you’re working with (e.g., “a Seurat object with 8,000 cells from wound healing tissue”)

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.4 The Slash Commands
Type these in the Claude Code sidebar:



Command
When to use it




/new-project
Starting a new analysis project


/new-plan
Planning a multi-session analysis


/learn-code
Understanding a script you didn’t write


/audit-script
Before sending a script for review


/done
At the end of every working session

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.5 A Typical Day
Here’s what a typical working session looks like:

Open your project in Positron (File → Open Folder)
Check the session log in .claude/CLAUDE.md to see where you left off
Work on your analysis — ask Claude Code for help as needed
Run /done at the end of the session to commit and update the log

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
section: 4.6 Before You Send Code for Review
text:
4.6 Before You Send Code for Review
Before sending any script to Valerie or a collaborator for review, always run:
/audit-script
This catches bugs, analytical issues, and reproducibility problems before anyone else sees them. It will produce a report in outputs/audit_reports/.






ImportantRunning /audit-script before review is lab policy



Think of it like spell-checking before sending an email — it’s a quick step that catches problems and shows you’ve thought carefully about your work.

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.7 Getting Help
If Claude Code can’t help with something, ask in the lab Discord or email valerie.horsley@yale.edu.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.1 Why Positron?
In Chapter 3, you used Positron to set up a project, write code in the editor, and run it in the console. This chapter digs deeper into how Positron works and how to use it effectively for daily analysis work. We’ll cover what each panel does, how to navigate your project, and habits that make working smoother.
Positron is our IDE (Integrated Development Environment) — it’s where you write code, explore data, run analyses, and manage projects. Built by Posit (the same team behind RStudio), Positron keeps the best RStudio-style data science features — the plotting pane, environment viewer, data viewer — while adding the best parts of VS Code, including Git integration and Claude Code.
If you’ve used RStudio before, Positron will feel familiar. You still get an R console, a variable explorer, a data viewer, and a file browser. A few features that make it particularly useful for our work:
Full support for R and Python. Most of our work is in R (Seurat, DESeq2), but some tools require Python. In Positron, you can run R and Python sessions side by side, each with its own console and environment.
An excellent Data Viewer. Click any dataframe in the Variables pane and it opens in an interactive viewer where you can sort, filter, and search. For Seurat metadata tables, this is invaluable.
Claude Code integration. The Claude Code sidebar is built directly into Positron — you can ask for help, run lab skills, and get code reviewed right alongside your scripts.

title: 5  Positron
section: 5.2 The Bottom Panel: Console, Terminal, and Variables
text:
5.2 The Bottom Panel: Console, Terminal, and Variables
The bottom of the Positron window is where most interactive work happens. It contains tabs for your R console and terminal.

5.2.1 Console vs Terminal
These are the two most important tabs, and they do fundamentally different things.
The Console is your R session. When you press Cmd+Enter (Mac) or Ctrl+Enter (Windows) to run a code chunk, it executes here. This is where you see R output, error messages, and package loading messages.
The Terminal is a system shell — the same as Terminal.app on macOS or PowerShell on Windows, but built into Positron. Use it for shell commands: running git, rendering Quarto documents, or anything you’d normally do at the command line.
The key distinction: R code goes in the Console, shell commands go in the Terminal. If Claude Code tells you to run git add ., that goes in the Terminal. If it tells you to run library(Seurat), that goes in the Console.






Tip



Mixing these up is a common early mistake. You’ll get object not found if you type a shell command into the R console, or command not found if you type R code into the terminal.








NoteWindows Users (PowerShell terminal)



On Windows, Positron’s integrated terminal is PowerShell, not bash/zsh. Basic navigation commands work the same (cd, ls, mkdir), but some Unix-style commands used in this guide may differ. Key differences:

Directory listing: ls works but behaves slightly differently than on Mac
File paths: Use backslashes (\) in PowerShell, or forward slashes (/) which also work in most contexts
Conda activation: If conda activate doesn’t work, run conda init powershell in PowerShell first, then restart

When in doubt, commands shown in this book work on both platforms unless otherwise noted.




5.2.2 Managing Multiple Sessions
As you work, the bottom panel accumulates tabs — you might have an R console and one or two terminals open. Each appears as a labeled tab. Click a tab to switch to that session.
Be careful with multiple R sessions: whichever console tab is visible is where Cmd+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter) sends your code. If you have two R sessions open and you’re looking at the wrong one, your code runs in a session that might not have the objects you expect.


5.2.3 The Variables Pane
The Variables pane shows every object in your current R session — Seurat objects with their dimensions, dataframes with row and column counts, vectors, lists. Click any dataframe to open it in the Data Viewer. The pane updates in real time as you create, modify, or remove objects.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.3 The Data Viewer
The Data Viewer is one of Positron’s most useful features for data analysis work. There are two ways to open it:

Click any dataframe in the Variables pane
Click a .csv or .tsv file in the File Explorer — Positron opens it directly in the viewer

Once open, the Data Viewer lets you:

Sort by clicking column headers
Filter rows using the filter bar
Search for specific values across the table
See distributions via column histograms at the top of each numeric column







TipSeurat Objects



You can’t view a Seurat object directly in the Data Viewer — it’s a complex nested structure. To inspect cell metadata, run View(seu@meta.data) in the console. This opens just the metadata table in the viewer.


During exploratory analysis, keep the Data Viewer open constantly — check data after each transformation, look for unexpected values, verify that joins worked correctly.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.4 Navigating Your Project

5.4.1 The File Explorer and Outline
The File Explorer in the left sidebar shows all files and folders in your project folder. Click files to open them — .csv and .tsv files open in the Data Viewer; code files open in the editor. Right-click to create, rename, or delete files.
The Outline panel shows the structure of the current document — for .Rmd files, it lists headings and code chunks. Click any item to jump to that section. Once your scripts get longer, the Outline is essential for navigation.


5.4.2 Opening Files Quickly
Press Cmd+P (Mac) or Ctrl+P (Windows) to open the Quick Open bar — start typing part of a filename and Positron shows matching files. Much faster than clicking through the File Explorer once your project has many files.


5.4.3 Working with Multiple Files
Files open as tabs in the editor. Drag a tab to the right edge to split the view and see two files side by side — useful when reading one script’s outputs while writing code that depends on them.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.5 Search
Press Cmd+Shift+F (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+F (Windows) to search across every file in your project. Useful for finding where a function is defined, locating every use of a variable, or searching for a specific column name across all your scripts.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.6 The Command Palette
The Command Palette is Positron’s universal command search. Open it with Cmd+Shift+P (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows), then start typing what you want to do. It searches through every available command — switching R versions, changing settings, toggling panels.
You’ll encounter it most often when Claude Code gives you instructions like “open the Command Palette and type ‘R: Select R Binary’.” Rather than memorizing where every setting lives in menus, you type what you’re looking for.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.7 The Status Bar
The status bar runs along the very bottom of the Positron window. It shows:

Active R version (e.g., “R 4.4.2”) — click to change
Current Git branch (e.g., “main”)
Line and column number for your cursor position

When something isn’t working — packages seem missing, or code behaves unexpectedly — check the status bar first. It often reveals the problem: you’re running the wrong R version.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.8 Essential Keyboard Shortcuts



Action
Mac
Windows




Run current line/selection
Cmd+Enter
Ctrl+Enter


Run current chunk
Cmd+Shift+Enter
Ctrl+Shift+Enter


Open Command Palette
Cmd+Shift+P
Ctrl+Shift+P


Quick file open
Cmd+P
Ctrl+P


Toggle terminal
Cmd+`
Ctrl+`


Save file
Cmd+S
Ctrl+S


Project-wide search
Cmd+Shift+F
Ctrl+Shift+F



Cmd+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter) is the one you’ll use constantly. The Command Palette is how you access anything in Positron you’re not sure how to find.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.9 Working with R

5.9.1 Starting and Restarting R
An R session starts automatically when you run R code from an .Rmd file. If the project has renv set up, you’ll see a message like Project loaded. [renv 1.0.0] confirming project-specific packages are active.
Restarting R gives you a clean slate and is something you should do regularly. Click the restart icon (↻) in the Console toolbar, or press Cmd+Shift+0 (Mac) / Ctrl+Shift+0 (Windows).
Why restart? During interactive development, your R session accumulates objects — some created by your script, some typed manually in the console while exploring. Over time it gets hard to tell what’s in your script versus what you created on the fly. Restarting clears everything and lets you test whether your script is truly self-contained.
After restarting, run your script from the top. If it executes cleanly, your script has all the code it needs. If it fails with “object not found” errors, something is missing from the script.






WarningWorks interactively but fails on render?



This is a very common problem. You run chunks one by one and everything works, but when you render the document it fails. The cause is almost always an object you created manually in the console that isn’t in any chunk.
To diagnose: restart R, then run all chunks from the top. Whatever fails is missing from your script.




5.9.2 Setting the R Version
If you need to change which R version Positron uses:

Open the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P / Ctrl+Shift+P)
Type “R: Select R Binary”
Choose the version you want

This only needs to be done once per project — Positron remembers your choice.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.10 Source Control Basics
The Source Control panel (click the branch icon in the left sidebar, or Cmd+Shift+G / Ctrl+Shift+G) shows which files have changed since your last commit.
Basic workflow: 1. Make changes to your files 2. Open Source Control — review changed files 3. Stage files by clicking the + icon 4. Type a commit message 5. Click the checkmark to commit 6. Push via the three-dot menu (⋯) → Push






Note/done handles commits



Once you’re comfortable with the basics, the /done skill in Claude Code handles committing your work at the end of each session. But understanding the Source Control panel helps you verify that commits are correct.


See ?sec-git for a full introduction to Git and GitHub.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.11 When Things Go Wrong

5.11.1 A Panel Disappeared
Go to the View menu and look for the panel you need — View → Terminal, View → Console, etc. Or use the Command Palette and type the panel name.


5.11.2 Console Won’t Start
Check the Output panel (View → Output) and select “R” from the dropdown — this shows diagnostic messages that often explain the actual error. Also check the status bar to verify the R path is correct. If all else fails, restart Positron.


5.11.3 Wrong R Version Running
Check the status bar at the bottom. If it’s wrong, use the Command Palette: “R: Select R Binary”.


5.11.4 Everything Feels Broken
Close Positron completely and reopen your project. This fixes most transient issues — stuck processes, stale caches, panels that won’t respond.
On Windows, if Positron hangs on close, use Task Manager to end the process before reopening.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.12 Extensions
Positron comes with most of what you need built in. A few extensions worth adding:

Quarto — for .qmd file support (may already be installed)
Jupyter — for Python notebook support
Claude Code — the AI coding assistant, if not already installed

Install extensions through the Extensions panel (Cmd+Shift+X / Ctrl+Shift+X), search for the name, and click Install.






TipStart Minimal



Don’t install a dozen extensions on day one. Start with the essentials above and add more only when you encounter a specific need.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.1 The Two Rules
Imagine opening a project you haven’t touched in six months. Or inheriting a colleague’s analysis when they rotate out of the lab. You need to figure out: where’s the raw data? Which script produces which figure? What’s the current version of the analysis? Is that CSV file something I downloaded, or something a script generated?
A well-organized project answers these questions through its structure alone. You shouldn’t need to read code or ask someone — the folder layout and naming conventions should tell you what’s what. This chapter describes the conventions we use in the Horsley Lab to make that possible.
In Chapter 3, you created a project using /new-project. This chapter explains that structure formally and covers how it scales as projects grow.
Every analysis project has two types of files, and the entire organizational system follows from keeping them separate:
This separation is the foundation. When you know that everything in data/ came from outside and everything in outputs/ was generated by code, you can always trace where a file came from and how to recreate it.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
Inputs are sacred. Data that comes from outside the project — sequencing results, collaborator files, public datasets — goes in data/ and is never modified by your scripts. If you need a cleaned version, your script reads the original, transforms it, and saves the result as an output.
Outputs are disposable. Everything your scripts produce — processed Seurat objects, figures, tables, rendered reports — goes in outputs/ and can always be regenerated by rerunning the script that created it.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.2 Anatomy of a Lab Project
Here’s what a typical Horsley Lab project looks like:
my-project/
├── .claude/                  # Claude Code project config
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── data/                     # External inputs — scripts never write here
│   ├── raw/                  # Original files, never modified
│   └── processed/            # Cleaned/transformed data
├── scripts/                  # R analysis scripts (.Rmd)
│   └── scratch/              # Exploratory, one-off analyses
├── outputs/                  # All generated outputs
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/                     # Notes, protocols, manuscript drafts
├── renv.lock                 # R package versions
├── .gitignore
└── README.md
The /new-project skill creates this entire structure for you — creating directories, initializing git, setting up renv, and generating a .claude/CLAUDE.md with your project’s conventions. But it’s worth understanding what each piece does.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.3 data/ — Your Inputs Are Sacred
The data/ folder holds files that come from outside the project — things your scripts read but never write to:
data/
├── raw/
│   ├── counts_matrix.h5          # From the sequencing core
│   ├── sample_metadata.xlsx      # From a collaborator
│   └── README.md                 # Documents where each file came from
└── processed/
    ├── seu_filtered.rds          # Seurat object after QC
    └── metadata_annotated.csv    # Cleaned metadata
The raw/ subdirectory holds truly original files — Cell Ranger output, sequencing data from the core, metadata spreadsheets from collaborators, reference files downloaded from databases. Scripts never write to data/raw/.
The processed/ subdirectory is for cleaned or transformed data that you trust enough to use as inputs in later scripts — for example, a filtered Seurat object that took 30 minutes to generate, or a metadata table you spent time cleaning by hand. These are still inputs, not outputs, so they live in data/ rather than outputs/.
Document where each file came from. A data/raw/README.md is the simplest approach — note the source, date received, and any relevant details for each file. Six months from now, you’ll be grateful you wrote down which version of the genome reference you used, or which email attachment that metadata came from.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.4 scripts/ — Numbered Analysis Scripts
All analysis scripts live in scripts/, numbered to show their logical flow:
scripts/
├── 01_qc.Rmd
├── 02_normalization.Rmd
├── 03_clustering.Rmd
├── 04_annotation.Rmd
├── 05_de_analysis.Rmd
└── scratch/
    └── test_resolution_params.Rmd

6.4.1 Why .Rmd Files?
In the lab, all data analysis scripts are R Markdown documents (.Rmd), not plain .R scripts. R Markdown lets you combine code, results, and narrative explanation in a single file — so your analysis documents what it does and why as it runs. When you knit an .Rmd file, it produces an HTML report with your figures, tables, and text woven together.
Use plain .R files only for helper functions that you source() into your main scripts.


6.4.2 Why Numbers?
The two-digit prefix (01_, 02_, etc.) serves a simple purpose: when you look at the file explorer, scripts appear in the order of your analysis pipeline. Script 01 imports and does QC. Script 02 normalizes. Script 03 clusters. A new lab member can glance at the file list and understand the analysis flow.
The numbers indicate logical order, not strict dependencies. Script 05 might read outputs from scripts 01 and 03 — the numbering just helps you understand the overall structure at a glance.
When you add a new script, assign the next available number. If you archive or delete a script, don’t renumber the remaining ones — leave gaps. This avoids confusion with documentation that mentions the old numbers.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.5 outputs/ — Provenance Through Structure
Here’s the convention that makes everything traceable: every script gets a matching output folder. Script 03_clustering.Rmd writes all its outputs to outputs/03_clustering/:
outputs/
├── figures/
│   ├── 03_clustering/
│   │   ├── umap_clusters.pdf
│   │   └── cluster_sizes.pdf
│   └── 05_de_analysis/
│       └── volcano_plot.pdf
├── tables/
│   ├── 03_clustering/
│   │   └── cluster_summary.csv
│   └── 05_de_analysis/
│       └── de_results.csv
└── 03_clustering/
    ├── seu_clustered.rds
    └── BUILD_INFO.txt
This structure encodes provenance automatically. When you see a file in outputs/03_clustering/, you know exactly which script created it. The Seurat object, figures, and tables from each script are clearly associated with that script.
Output ownership is strict: a script writes only to its own output folder, never to another script’s. If script 05 needs a modified version of something script 03 produced, it reads script 03’s output and saves its own version in outputs/05_de_analysis/.

6.5.1 BUILD_INFO.txt
Every numbered script writes a BUILD_INFO.txt to its output folder as its last action:
script: 03_clustering.Rmd
commit: a1b2c3d
date: 2024-03-20 15:30:00
This answers a question that comes up constantly: “When was this output last regenerated, and from what version of the code?” If downstream figures look wrong, you can check the upstream folder’s BUILD_INFO.txt to see whether the Seurat object was generated from current code or something stale.
Add this to the end of every numbered script:
# Write build info
build_info <- data.frame(
  script = "03_clustering.Rmd",
  commit = system("git rev-parse --short HEAD", intern = TRUE),
  date = as.character(Sys.time())
)
write.csv(build_info, file.path(dir_out, "BUILD_INFO.txt"), row.names = FALSE)


6.5.2 Setting Up Output Paths
In your setup chunk, define paths that match this structure:
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(Seurat)

# This script's output folder
dir_out <- here::here("outputs", "03_clustering")
dir.create(dir_out, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create(here::here("outputs", "figures", "03_clustering"), 
           recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Input paths
path_seu <- here::here("outputs", "02_normalization", "seu_normalized.rds")
Then save all outputs to dir_out:
# Save Seurat object
saveRDS(seu, file.path(dir_out, "seu_clustered.rds"))

# Save figures
ggsave(
  here::here("outputs", "figures", "03_clustering", "umap_clusters.pdf"),
  p_umap, width = 6, height = 5
)

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.6 How Scripts Connect
Scripts read from two places: data/ (external inputs) and outputs/ (outputs from other scripts). Group all input reads at the top of each script, with comments distinguishing external data from other scripts’ outputs:
# --- Inputs (from other scripts) ---
seu <- readRDS(here("outputs/02_normalization/seu_normalized.rds"))

# --- Inputs (external data) ---
metadata <- read_csv(here("data/raw/sample_metadata.csv"))
marker_genes <- read_csv(here("data/raw/published_skin_markers.csv"))
Reading the top of any script shows exactly what it depends on and where those files come from.
Here’s what a typical Horsley Lab scRNAseq pipeline looks like as a dependency diagram:





flowchart LR
    data["data/raw/*"] --> s01["01_qc"]
    s01 --> s02["02_normalization"]
    s02 --> s03["03_clustering"]
    s03 --> s04["04_annotation"]
    s04 --> s05["05_de_analysis"]
    data --> s05

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.7 The Scratch Directory
Not everything you write is part of the main analysis pipeline. Sometimes you need to test a clustering resolution, try a new visualization, or run a quick sanity check. That’s what scripts/scratch/ is for:
scripts/
├── 01_qc.Rmd
├── 02_normalization.Rmd
├── scratch/
│   ├── test_resolution_params.Rmd
│   └── compare_normalization_methods.Rmd
Scratch scripts follow relaxed rules:

No number prefixes or BUILD_INFO.txt required
No other script depends on them — this is the critical rule. Scratch scripts can read from any outputs/ folder, but nothing outside of scratch/ reads from scratch outputs. This is a one-way dependency.
Clean them out at the end of each session — use /done to consolidate useful scratch code into numbered scripts







TipWhen scratch becomes permanent



If a scratch analysis proves valuable, promote it to a numbered script in the main directory. Use /done at the end of each session — it will check scripts/scratch/ and help you consolidate useful code into the right numbered script.

title: 6  Project Organization
section: 6.8 Growing a Project: Flat vs. Sectioned
text:
6.8 Growing a Project: Flat vs. Sectioned
The structure shown above is a flat layout — all scripts in one directory, one numbering sequence. This works well for most projects.
When a project grows to include multiple distinct analyses — say, scRNAseq and spatial transcriptomics from the same experiment — a flat layout gets unwieldy. Switch to a sectioned layout:
project/
├── scripts/
│   ├── scrna/
│   │   ├── 01_qc.Rmd
│   │   ├── 02_normalization.Rmd
│   │   └── 03_clustering.Rmd
│   ├── spatial/
│   │   ├── 01_load_visium.Rmd
│   │   └── 02_spatial_de.Rmd
│   ├── combined/
│   │   └── 01_integration.Rmd
│   └── scratch/
├── data/
│   ├── scrna/
│   └── spatial/
└── outputs/
    ├── scrna/
    │   ├── 01_qc/
    │   └── 02_normalization/
    ├── spatial/
    │   └── 01_load_visium/
    └── combined/
        └── 01_integration/
A few things to notice:

Numbering restarts at 01_ in each section — each analytical thread has its own sequence
Data and outputs mirror the section structure — scripts/scrna/ has a corresponding data/scrna/ and outputs/scrna/
Cross-section scripts like combined/01_integration.Rmd can read from any section’s outputs/ folder

Start flat. Switch to sectioned when you have more than about ten scripts or two distinct analytical threads.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.9 Script Lifecycle
Track the status of each script with a status field in the YAML frontmatter:
---
title: "03 — Clustering"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
status: development
output: html_document
---







Status
Meaning




development
In active development; outputs may change


finalized
Outputs are publication-ready; modify only deliberately


deprecated
Superseded by a newer script; kept for reference



When a script is superseded, move it to scripts/old/ and add a note in the YAML:
---
title: "Old Clustering Approach"
status: deprecated
deprecated_by: 03_clustering.Rmd
output: html_document
---

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.10 Naming Conventions
Good file names are lowercase, descriptive, and use underscores:

Scripts: 03_clustering.Rmd, not 3-Clustering.Rmd or clustering final.Rmd
Output files: seu_clustered.rds, not data.rds or output_final_v2.rds
Figures: umap_cell_types.pdf, not figure1.pdf

Avoid spaces (they cause problems in terminal commands), generic names (results.csv), and date prefixes on files — let git track versions instead.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.11 Version Control
Your .gitignore should include:
# Raw data (often large)
data/raw/

# Generated outputs (reproducible from scripts)
outputs/

# R artifacts
.Rhistory
.RData
renv/library/
renv/staging/

# Quarto rendering
*_files/
.quarto/

# OS and IDE files
.DS_Store
.positron/

# Secrets
.env
*.pem
credentials.json
Whether to commit data/raw/ depends on file sizes. Small files (a few MB) can be committed so the project is self-contained. Large files (Cell Ranger output, raw FASTQ) should be gitignored, with a data/raw/README.md documenting where to get them.
See ?sec-git for a full introduction to version control.

title: 6  Project Organization
text:
6.12 A Complete Example
Here’s a well-organized wound healing scRNAseq project:
wound_healing_scrna/
├── .claude/
│   ├── CLAUDE.md
│   └── WOUND_HEALING_SCRNA_PLAN.md   # Planning doc
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   │   ├── README.md                 # Documents data sources
│   │   ├── WH_001/                   # Cell Ranger output, sample 1
│   │   ├── WH_002/                   # Cell Ranger output, sample 2
│   │   └── sample_metadata.csv       # Experimental design
│   └── processed/
│       └── seu_merged.rds            # Merged object (slow to generate)
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_qc.Rmd                     # status: finalized
│   ├── 02_normalization.Rmd          # status: finalized
│   ├── 03_clustering.Rmd             # status: finalized
│   ├── 04_annotation.Rmd             # status: development
│   ├── 05_de_analysis.Rmd            # status: development
│   ├── scratch/
│   │   └── test_harmony_params.Rmd
│   └── old/
│       └── 03_clustering_v1.Rmd      # status: deprecated
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/
│   │   ├── 01_qc/
│   │   │   ├── violin_nfeature.pdf
│   │   │   └── violin_pct_mt.pdf
│   │   └── 03_clustering/
│   │       └── umap_clusters.pdf
│   ├── tables/
│   │   └── 05_de_analysis/
│   │       └── de_results.csv
│   ├── 02_normalization/
│   │   ├── seu_normalized.rds
│   │   └── BUILD_INFO.txt
│   └── 03_clustering/
│       ├── seu_clustered.rds
│       └── BUILD_INFO.txt
├── docs/
│   └── cell_type_marker_notes.md
├── renv.lock
├── .gitignore
└── README.md
From this structure, anyone can understand the project without reading a single line of code:

Where did the data come from? Check data/raw/README.md
What’s the analysis pipeline? Read the numbered scripts in order
Which script produced seu_clustered.rds? It’s in outputs/03_clustering/, so script 03 made it
Is the analysis finalized? Check the status field — scripts 01–03 are finalized, 04–05 are still in development
What code version produced these outputs? Check BUILD_INFO.txt in each output folder
How do I reproduce everything? Restore packages with renv::restore(), then run the scripts in order

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.1 The Problem renv Solves
In Chapter 3, you ran renv::init(), installed a few packages, and ran renv::snapshot() to lock everything down. It worked — but what actually happened? Where did those packages go? What is renv.lock recording?
This chapter answers those questions. Managing R packages effectively requires one key tool: renv. It gives each project a self-contained, reproducible package library — so installing something for one project can’t break another, and your collaborators can reproduce your exact environment six months from now.
Without renv, every R project on your computer shares the same package library. Install a new version of ggplot2 for one project, and every other project gets it too. This causes two problems:
renv solves both problems by giving each project its own private package library, and recording the exact version of every package in a lockfile (renv.lock). Anyone who clones your project can run renv::restore() and get the exact same packages you had.

title: 7  R and renv
text:
Broken projects — updating a package for Project A might break something in Project B that depended on the old version
Irreproducibility — if you share your code with a collaborator, they might have different package versions installed and get different results

title: 7  R and renv
section: 7.2 What renv Created in Your Project
text:
7.2 What renv Created in Your Project
When you ran renv::init(), renv created several files:
renv/ — your project’s private package library. When you install packages, they go here, not into a shared system library.
renv.lock — a snapshot of every package in your project library: names, exact versions, and where they came from (CRAN, Bioconductor, GitHub). This is the key file for reproducibility.
.Rprofile — runs automatically when R starts in this folder. It activates renv so R uses your project library instead of the system library. This is why you see Project loaded. [renv 1.0.0] in the console when you open the project in Positron.

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.3 Installing Packages
For most packages, install.packages() still works — renv intercepts the call and installs into your project library. But renv::install() is more versatile because it understands multiple sources:
# Install from CRAN (either method works)
install.packages("ggplot2")
renv::install("ggplot2")

# Install from Bioconductor (must use bioc:: prefix)
renv::install("bioc::DESeq2")
renv::install("bioc::SingleCellExperiment")

# Install from GitHub
renv::install("satijalab/seurat")

# Install a specific version
renv::install("ggplot2@3.4.0")
The bioc:: prefix is important. Bioconductor is a separate repository of R packages for bioinformatics — things like DESeq2 for differential expression, SingleCellExperiment for single-cell data structures, and hundreds of other genomics tools. These packages aren’t on CRAN, so you need the prefix to tell renv where to find them.
See ?sec-r-packages for a guide to common R packages used in the Horsley Lab.






TipNot sure which packages you need?



Ask Claude Code in Positron:

I’m starting a scRNAseq analysis in my renv project. I need to run Seurat, do differential expression, and make publication-quality figures. What packages do I need and how do I install them?

Claude will recommend the right packages and generate the correct renv::install() commands with the bioc:: prefix for Bioconductor packages.

title: 7  R and renv
section: 7.4 The Core Loop: Install → Snapshot → Commit
text:
7.4 The Core Loop: Install → Snapshot → Commit
After installing packages, the most important habit is snapshot:
renv::snapshot()
This updates renv.lock with the exact versions of everything in your project library. After snapshotting, commit the updated renv.lock to git. This is the cycle you’ll repeat throughout a project:

Install or update a package
Run renv::snapshot()
Commit renv.lock to git

Why does this matter? Imagine a lab member clones your project from GitHub six months from now. The R code is all there, but without the right packages at the right versions, nothing runs. When they run:
renv::restore()
renv reads renv.lock and installs every package at the exact version you recorded. They get the same environment you had — not whatever happens to be current on CRAN that day.

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.5 Checking Status
Before committing your work, check whether your library and lockfile are in sync:
renv::status()
This tells you if any packages are installed but not recorded in the lockfile (you need to snapshot()), or missing from your library (you need to restore()).
The most common message is “The project is out-of-sync.” This sounds alarming but usually just means you installed something and forgot to snapshot, or you pulled changes from git that updated the lockfile and haven’t restored yet.

Packages installed but not in lockfile → run renv::snapshot()
Packages in lockfile but not installed → run renv::restore()

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.6 Updating Packages
Packages get updates — bug fixes, new features, performance improvements. To update everything in your project:
renv::update()
Or update a specific package:
renv::update("Seurat")
Useful habit: snapshot and commit before updating. That way, if an update breaks something, you can restore the previous state:
renv::restore()
This reinstalls packages at the versions in renv.lock, effectively undoing the update.

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.7 What to Commit to Git
Include in git:

renv.lock — the most important file; records exact package versions
renv/activate.R — the activation script
.Rprofile — auto-activates renv when R starts

Exclude from git (already in your .gitignore):

renv/library/ — the actual installed packages (too large and platform-specific)
renv/staging/ — temporary files during installation

The default renv setup handles these .gitignore entries automatically when you run renv::init().

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.8 Troubleshooting

7.8.1 “The project is out-of-sync”
Run renv::status() to see the details, then either renv::snapshot() or renv::restore() depending on the direction of the mismatch.


7.8.2 Package installation fails
Sometimes a package won’t install, especially packages with compiled code. Try:
renv::install("package-name", rebuild = TRUE)
For Bioconductor packages, make sure your R version is compatible with the Bioconductor release you need.






TipGetting help with installation errors



Paste the full error message into Claude Code in Positron:

I’m trying to install DESeq2 with renv::install("bioc::DESeq2") but getting errors. Here’s the output: [paste error]. Can you help me figure out what’s wrong?

Claude can check whether the error is a version mismatch, a missing system library, or a network issue, and suggest the fix.




7.8.3 renv is slow
The first time you run renv::restore() on a new machine, renv downloads and installs every package from scratch. This takes a while for projects with many packages — Seurat alone has dozens of dependencies. Be patient. Subsequent operations are faster because renv maintains a global cache — packages shared across projects only download once.


7.8.4 Starting fresh
If things are really broken:
renv::deactivate()
Then in the terminal:
rm -rf renv renv.lock .Rprofile
Then reinitialize:
renv::init()






Warning



This removes your lockfile and the record of which package versions you had. Copy renv.lock somewhere safe before doing this if you need to preserve that information.

title: 7  R and renv
text:
7.9 Quick Reference







Task
Command




Initialize renv
renv::init()


Install from CRAN
install.packages("pkg") or renv::install("pkg")


Install from Bioconductor
renv::install("bioc::pkg")


Install from GitHub
renv::install("user/repo")


Snapshot
renv::snapshot()


Restore
renv::restore()


Check status
renv::status()


Update all packages
renv::update()


Update one package
renv::update("pkg")

title: Appendix C — Lab Skills Reference
text:
C.1 Slash Commands
The Horsley Lab has a set of custom Claude Code skills that teach Claude our conventions and workflows. This appendix documents what each skill does, when to use it, and how to use it effectively.
Skills are installed from the Horsleylab GitHub organization. See Chapter 2 for install instructions.
These skills are invoked by typing a command in the Claude Code sidebar or terminal.

title: Appendix C — Lab Skills Reference
text:
C.1.1 /new-project
When to use: At the start of any new analysis project.
What it does: Scaffolds a complete project with the correct Horsley Lab folder structure, initializes renv, sets up git, creates a CLAUDE.md, and optionally connects to GitHub.
How to use it:

Create a folder for your project and open it in Positron
Type /new-project in the Claude Code sidebar
Answer the questions — project type, name, description, whether you’ll use the Bouchet cluster, GitHub settings

Claude will create everything in one step. You’ll end up with:
project-name/
├── data/raw/
├── data/processed/
├── scripts/scratch/
├── outputs/figures/
├── outputs/tables/
├── docs/
├── .claude/CLAUDE.md
├── renv.lock
└── README.md
Example:
/new-project



C.1.2 /new-plan
When to use: When starting a multi-session analysis that needs tracking — anything that will take more than one or two sessions to complete.
What it does: Creates a planning document in .claude/ that tracks phases, scripts, key decisions, and next steps. At the end of each session, /done updates this document automatically.
How to use it:
/new-plan wound healing scRNAseq annotation
Claude will ask whether you want a data science or general style plan, and whether to use checkboxes or a status table. It creates a .md file in .claude/ and registers it in your CLAUDE.md.
Example planning document:
# Wound Healing scRNAseq Annotation Plan

## Status
| Phase | Description | Status |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | QC and filtering | ✅ Done |
| 2 | Normalization and clustering | 🔄 In progress |
| 3 | Cell type annotation | ⬜ Not started |

## Key Decisions
- 2024-03-15 — Excluded sample WH_003 (482 cells, below 500 threshold)
- 2024-03-18 — Using log-normalize not SCTransform; CellChat requires RNA assay

## Working Notes
Testing resolutions 0.4–0.8 for clustering. UMAP looks good at 0.5.



C.1.3 /audit-script
When to use: Before sending any script to Valerie or a collaborator for review. Also useful when you’re not sure if your analysis is correct.
What it does: Systematically reviews a script for bugs, analytical issues, data handling problems, style, and reproducibility gaps. Produces a structured report saved to outputs/audit_reports/.






ImportantLab policy



Run /audit-script before sending any script for review. Think of it like spell-checking before sending an email — it catches problems and shows you’ve thought carefully about your work.


Three modes:

Thorough — Collaborative, section-by-section. Claude walks through the script with you, asks questions, and you run code together. Best for learning or for scripts with complex analytical choices.
Fast — Claude reads the whole script independently and presents findings for discussion. Best for a quick pre-review before meeting with Valerie.
Report only — Claude audits independently and saves a report. Best for a quick quality check you’ll read on your own.

How to use it:
/audit-script
Claude will ask which script to audit, which mode, and whether you have specific concerns.
What it checks:

Correctness — bugs, wrong variable references, functions used incorrectly
Analytical reasoning — is the statistical test appropriate? are thresholds justified?
Data handling — silent cell drops, wrong assay slot, unvalidated joins
Style — clarity, naming, comments, magic numbers
Reproducibility — hardcoded paths, missing set.seed(), undocumented inputs

Example findings:
BUG-1: Wrong assay slot for FindMarkers
- Lines: 47-50
- Description: DefaultAssay is SCT but FindMarkers requires RNA counts for DESeq2 test
- Recommendation: Add DefaultAssay(seu) <- "RNA" before FindMarkers()

WARNING-1: Missing set.seed() before RunUMAP
- Lines: 62
- Description: UMAP is stochastic — results will differ between runs
- Recommendation: Add set.seed(42) before RunUMAP()



C.1.4 /learn-code
When to use: When you want to understand a script — one someone else wrote, one from a paper, or one you wrote a while ago and have forgotten.
What it does: Walks through a script interactively, section by section, covering what the code does mechanically, why it’s organized that way, and what analytical choices are embedded in it. Generates study notes at the end.
Three focus modes:

Balanced (default) — equal attention to mechanics and analytical reasoning
Focus on mechanics — more time on syntax and functions; good for building coding fluency
Focus on analysis — skim syntax, dive deep into analytical choices; good if you already know R

How to use it:
/learn-code
Claude will ask which script to walk through and your experience level, then guide you through it section by section. You’ll be asked to run code in your console and predict what it produces before Claude explains it.
At the end, Claude generates study notes you can save to outputs/ — a summary of what the script does, key concepts learned, analytical decisions made, and questions to think about.



C.1.5 /done
When to use: At the end of every working session.
What it does: Summarizes what you did, updates the session log in CLAUDE.md, updates any planning documents, consolidates scratch files, checks renv, and commits your work to git.
How to use it:
/done
Claude will:

List what was completed this session
Append a session log entry to .claude/CLAUDE.md
Update any relevant planning documents
Check scripts/scratch/ for files to consolidate
Check if renv needs a snapshot
Propose a git commit with the session’s files
Offer to push to GitHub

Example session log entry:
### 2024-03-20 — Clustering and annotation

- **Plans:** WOUND_HEALING_SCRNA_PLAN.md
- **Work:** Tested resolutions 0.4-0.8, chose 0.5 (14 clusters). Started cell type
  annotation using published skin markers.
- **Next:** Finish annotating ambiguous clusters 7 and 11. Run FindMarkers for
  cluster markers.






Tip



Make /done a habit. The session log means you always know exactly where you left off, even after a long break.

title: Appendix C — Lab Skills Reference
text:
C.2 Auto-Loading Skills
These skills load automatically when Claude detects a relevant task. You don’t need to invoke them manually.

C.2.1 horsley-lab-conventions
When it loads: When setting up a new project or when Claude detects you’re working without a clear folder structure.
What it does: Tells Claude our standard project structure, script naming conventions, data rules, and output conventions. Ensures Claude always organizes projects the Horsley Lab way.
Key conventions it enforces:

Numbered scripts (01_, 02_, etc.) run in order and reproduce all results
Scratch files in scripts/scratch/ are exploratory — consolidate before finishing
Raw data in data/raw/ is read-only — scripts never write there
All outputs go in outputs/ with descriptive names




C.2.2 data-handling
When it loads: When writing data manipulation code, analysis pipelines, or .Rmd scripts that process biological datasets.
What it does: Applies Horsley Lab best practices for data handling automatically. Ensures Claude always shows data summaries at key steps, annotates analytical decisions, and validates data to prevent silent data loss.
Key behaviors it triggers:

Reports cell/row counts after every filter, join, or subset
Checks active assay before any Seurat operation
Annotates normalization choices, QC thresholds, and clustering resolutions with rationale
Flags silent data loss risks (inner joins, NA removal, factor level drops)
Stops to ask before making important analytical choices

Example — with data-handling active, Claude writes:
# Filter low-quality cells: nFeature_RNA > 200 removes empty droplets,
# < 6000 removes likely doublets; percent.mt < 20 removes dying cells
# Thresholds chosen based on QC plots from 01_qc.Rmd
seu <- subset(seu, subset = nFeature_RNA > 200 &
                             nFeature_RNA < 6000 &
                             percent.mt < 20)
cat("After QC filter:", ncol(seu), "cells retained\n")
Instead of just:
seu <- subset(seu, subset = nFeature_RNA > 200 & nFeature_RNA < 6000 & percent.mt < 20)



C.2.3 cleanup-scripts
When it loads: When finishing a session, when scripts/scratch/ has accumulated files, or when you say “clean up scripts” or “wrap up this session.”
What it does: Checks scripts/scratch/ for working files that need consolidation into numbered .Rmd scripts, flags unnumbered scripts that should be numbered, and verifies script-output correspondence.






Note



/done includes script cleanup automatically. If you use /done at the end of every session, you don’t need to invoke cleanup-scripts separately.

title: Appendix C — Lab Skills Reference
section: C.3 Tips for Getting the Most from Skills
text:
C.3 Tips for Getting the Most from Skills
Be specific when invoking slash commands. You can give context directly:
/new-plan — this is for the adipocyte EV scRNAseq analysis, data science style
/audit-script — I'm worried about the DE section, we switched assays midway through
Skills work in Claude Code only, not the chat. All slash commands require Claude Code (Positron sidebar or terminal). They won’t work in the regular claude.ai chat interface.
Skills update automatically. When you restart Claude Code, the latest version of the lab skills is pulled from GitHub automatically. You don’t need to reinstall.

title: Appendix C — Lab Skills Reference
section: C.4 Suggesting New Skills or Reporting Problems
text:
C.4 Suggesting New Skills or Reporting Problems
If a skill does something wrong, doesn’t handle a situation you ran into, or you have an idea for a new skill:

Open an issue at github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills
Or email valerie.horsley@yale.edu

Include which skill was involved, what happened, and what you expected.
